title: Bounded Long-Running Project Conductor for Agent Teams
thesis: Long agent-driven software missions fail when one agent tries to hold the entire plan, execution runs without checkpoints, and process memory is lost between sessions. A bounded, queue-based project conductor externalizes state, executes dependency-ordered slices in isolated sessions, and feeds telemetry into a proposal-only self-improvement loop with mandatory human gates.
primary_reader: Engineering teams and agent operators who need to split multi-session software missions into bounded, reviewable, and governable slices.
intended_outcome: Readers can define a bounded conductor mission, decompose it into slices with authority envelopes, identify human handoff points, and apply the minimal schema to their own agent teams.
artifact_fit: The topic crosses orchestration, governance, and agent architecture; a durable reference note with a minimal schema is more useful than a one-off implementation guide.
smallest_viable_version: A 1,000-word version covering the three failure modes, the slice/queue model, the safety boundaries, and the minimal schema.
scope_risks: Tooling references (LangGraph, Temporal, AutoGen, CrewAI) may evolve quickly; the schema may need versioning as real implementations diverge.
alternatives_considered: A pure chat-based multi-agent framework guide, or a deep dive into a single vendor product; both were rejected in favor of a vendor-neutral, governance-first pattern.
claims: Long agent-driven projects fail predictably from context collapse, unbounded execution, and loss of durable process memory.
A bounded conductor externalizes plan, state, and telemetry so that no single agent session must hold the whole mission.
Dependency-ordered slices executed in isolated branches or worktrees reduce blast radius and make review gates practical.
Append-only telemetry supports a proposal-only self-improvement loop; the conductor must never self-authorize new scope, budget, or promotion.
Human handoffs are mandatory at mission approval, high-risk slices, review failures, improvement proposals, and final promotion.
The minimal mission schema is sufficient to describe scope, slices, authority envelopes, telemetry, and improvement proposals in a machine-readable form.
sanitized_summary: This proposal argues that long agent-driven software missions need a bounded, queue-based conductor that keeps plan and state outside any single agent session, runs dependency-ordered slices in isolated branches, reviews each output, and routes telemetry into a proposal-only self-improvement loop. It defines lifecycle states, safety boundaries, mandatory human handoff points, and a minimal machine-readable mission schema.

abstraction_examples.original: A client team had a multi-week project where one agent held the entire codebase plan, repeatedly lost context, and rewrote decisions made in earlier sessions.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: A multi-session software mission where state and decisions are not externalized, leading to repeated rework and lost context.

abstraction_examples.original: The conductor spawned a worker in a temporary worktree under /tmp/... to edit only the files listed in the slice authority envelope.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: Each slice runs in an isolated workspace restricted to the paths named in its authority envelope.

abstraction_examples.original: After all slices passed review, the human maintainer promoted the resulting package to the main branch and closed the mission.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: Final artifact promotion requires a human gate after all slices are reviewed and complete.

agent_involvement: This proposal was drafted by an AI agent from public sources and the associated public issue, with human review expected before publication.
